summary: We started our military journey at Fort Douglas before being shipped off to Fort McClellan, Alabama, for basic training. Fort Douglas was where our initial training took place, but it was nothing like the rigorous experience that awaited us at McClellan. It was the starting point of my transformation from a civilian to a soldier.

Fort McClellan in Alabama was where I underwent the toughest basic training of my life during World War II. It was there I met Tech Sergeant Meadows, who was the meanest and toughest man I’d ever encountered. The training was grueling from early morning until night, teaching us everything from handling rifles to hand-to-hand combat, and it was at McClellan that I was selected as a squad leader and later became a drill sergeant myself.

Tech Sergeant Meadows was the toughest, meanest, and foul-mouthed drill sergeant I met at Fort McClellan. His harsh words and strict discipline pushed me to become stronger and tougher, and I admired his leadership even though he was intimidating. Eventually, I became a drill sergeant like him, standing in front of recruits and commanding them every day.

Before joining the military, I was a Boy Scout, which gave me skills that proved valuable during basic training at Fort McClellan. My experience with shooting and using a compass helped me stand out, and the sergeant even selected me and Leo to be squad leaders. Being a Boy Scout laid a foundation that helped me adapt quickly to military life.

entity: Citadel, The Military College of South Carolina
summary: At Fort McClellan, I found myself training alongside men from prestigious military colleges like The Citadel. These men had three years of college and were well educated, but I, a high school dropout with a foul mouth, was now their drill sergeant. It was a strange but empowering experience to be in charge of such well-educated soldiers.

Alongside cadets from the Virginia Military Institute, I served as a drill sergeant at Fort McClellan. These men were well educated and had military training backgrounds, yet I was tasked with leading and training them despite my lack of formal education. It was a humbling but proud moment for me during my time at McClellan.

At Fort McClellan, I was a drill sergeant responsible for training men from places like Georgia Tech who had years of college education. Despite my background as a high school dropout, I was the one giving orders and teaching them discipline and combat skills. It was a remarkable experience that showed me how far I had come.

Jack Streeter was from my hometown and was assigned to a hut next to mine at Fort McClellan. He was older and didn’t want to be there; he even challenged the commanding officer to a fight to get shipped out. Eventually, he was sent to the 1st Infantry Division, which was heading to North Africa, while I stayed behind to train others.

Jack Streeter was assigned to the 1st Infantry Division, which was preparing for the invasion of North Africa during World War II. While I remained at Fort McClellan training recruits, he was eager to get into combat with this division. The 1st Infantry Division was one of the experienced combat units at the time.

On July 1st, after requesting to be sent into combat, I sailed for five days aboard the S.S. Manhattan to Europe. The ship was crowded, and many of us were seasick, but we were combat ready with our rifles, packs, and ammunition. The journey ended in Liverpool, England, where we began preparing for the invasion.

We landed in Liverpool, England, after a rough five-day trip on the S.S. Manhattan. The city was still suffering from wartime shortages, and local children were so hungry they dove into the water to grab candy and money we threw to them. It reminded me of the hardships during the Depression when I stood in line with my grandfather for food.

I was assigned to the 28th Infantry Division as a replacement platoon leader after arriving in England. I was promoted to staff sergeant on the spot and landed with them on Omaha Beach on July 16th. The division was known as 'The Bloody Bucket' by the Germans, and I quickly took command of eleven men in intense combat conditions.

I landed on Omaha Beach on July 16th, weeks after the initial invasion, carrying a heavy pack as I climbed the cliffs the soldiers had fought for. The beach was littered with wreckage and downed planes still smoking from the original assault. It was a grim introduction to combat and the realities of war.

In my platoon, the BAR man carried the Browning Automatic Rifle, which was crucial for providing rapid fire support during combat. This weapon shot very rapidly and required many clips of ammunition, making it a key part of our squad’s firepower. We relied on the BAR man to cover us while we engaged the enemy.

Most of the soldiers in my platoon carried the M1 Garand, an eight-round semi-automatic rifle that ejected clips as it fired. It was the standard weapon we trained with at Fort McClellan and used in combat across France. Knowing how to maintain and use the M1 was essential for survival.

After landing at Omaha Beach, we advanced through France, moving through hedgerows and flatlands under heavy German fire. We experienced intense combat near places like St. Lô and Paris, and the landscape was unfamiliar and challenging compared to our training in Alabama. France was the main battleground where I faced the harsh realities of war.

Our maps included Paris, and I kept an 'asshole compass' and a silk overlay of France up through Paris hidden on me in case of capture. Though we were not yet in Paris, it was a key reference point during our advance through France. The city represented a major objective in the campaign.

We heard rumors of a massive airstrike on St. Lô, and were ordered to withdraw before the bombing. Unfortunately, the message got mixed up, and we only withdrew six kilometers instead of twelve, leaving us within the bombing range. The bombing was devastating, with thousands of planes dropping bombs for hours, and many of our own men, including General McNair, were killed by friendly fire.

General Lesley J. McNair was killed during the massive bombing near St. Lô when our own planes mistakenly bombed within the perimeter of the smoke. It was a tragic incident that underscored the chaos and dangers of war, especially during large-scale operations. His death was a sobering moment for all of us.

Babe Ruth was mentioned in passing during my military experience as a cultural icon, but I didn’t have any direct connection with him. His name came up as part of the era’s popular culture that we soldiers remembered and talked about. He represented a piece of home and normal life during the war.

Chicago was my hometown, and it was where I knew people like Jack Streeter, who also ended up in the military. Thinking about Chicago while overseas reminded me of home and the life I left behind to fight in the war. It was a grounding thought during tough times.

Frank Sinatra was part of the soundtrack of my time during the war, representing the music and culture that kept our spirits up. Though I didn’t meet him, his songs were well known among the troops and brought a sense of normalcy and hope. He was a symbol of the era we lived through.
